nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef edges, water-break zones, and shaded reef-base areas; often found in low-flow pockets and sediment zones at reef margins

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Target feeding after lights out when tentacles are visible is generally advised — suitable prey includes copepods, brine shrimp nauplii, mysis shrimp, and small commercial coral foods. Regular weekly feedings have been reported to encourage polyp multiplication. Iodine and trace element supplementation is commonly recommended.

avoidWith: sweeper-tentacle corals placed in direct contact
large polyp corals that may overgrow

imageCandidates.notes: Isaurus cliftoni. Photo by Belinda Forbes (via GBIF), 2048x1549. Dense, vibrant olive-green colony of tubular snake polyps with many yellow feeding polyps open — tack-sharp, fills the frame, and clearly shows the diagnostic tuberculate stalks and open feeding polyps.

Isaurus tuberculatus. Photo by Robin White (via GBIF), 2048x1155. Sharp macro of polyps with feathery feeding tentacles fully extended — the signature nocturnal snake-polyp look described in the care notes.
Isaurus maculatus. Photo by Franz Anthony, 1024x683. Upright group of tubular polyps in a tide pool — sharp, adds a third distinct species to the genus gallery.
Isaurus cliftoni. Large high-resolution in-situ colony, 5472x3648. Grey-purple tubular polyps carpeting the reef base — shows the encrusting colony habit at scale (different colour form).
Isaurus tuberculatus. Photo by Robin White (via GBIF), 2048x1155. Second sharp shot of polyps with tentacles extended, from a slightly wider angle for variety.
Isaurus cliftoni. Photo by Ben Jobson (via GBIF), 2048x1536. Purple/blue polyps lying limp on the substrate — illustrates the deflated daytime posture and a distinct colour morph.

summary: The Isaurus Coral, also known as the Snake Polyp, is an uncommon zoanthid with long tubular stalks that lie limp and closed during the day and extend feathery feeding tentacles after lights go out. Regular target feeding after dark is generally advised for long-term success.
careNotes:
Isaurus (commonly called Snake Polyps or Curling Sea Mats) are among the more unusual zoanthids available in the reef trade. Several species are recognized, including I. tuberculatus, I. cliftoni, and I. maculatus, and are typically sold at genus level.

Distinctive nocturnal behavior: polyps tend to lie flat and closed during daylight hours, often appearing limp or deflated. After lights go out, the tubular stalks stand upright and the feathery feeding tentacles extend to capture prey. Feeding should be targeted during this nocturnal extension window.

Key care notes:
• Lighting: Moderate intensity is generally sufficient; some sources suggest lower PAR conditions (closer to 75 PAR) reflecting their natural shaded-reef-base habitat, while others note "moderate to high" is tolerated. Placement in mid to lower rockwork is commonly recommended.
• Flow: Medium water flow is advised to prevent detritus accumulation and facilitate tissue shedding, but direct high-flow laminar jets are best avoided.
• Feeding: Although photosynthetic, Isaurus have been reported to decline slowly in captivity when not fed regularly. Weekly target feeding after lights out with small zooplankton (copepods, brine shrimp nauplii, mysis) is strongly encouraged.
• Growth: In captivity, snake polyps may grow slowly for extended periods; some aquarists report colonies remaining static for years before suddenly spreading. Consistent water quality and feeding appear to be the key drivers.
• Palytoxin caution: As a member of the Zoanthidae, Isaurus may contain palytoxin. Appropriate handling precautions (gloves, eye protection, avoid mouth contact) are advised during fragging or water changes, consistent with all zoanthid handling best practices.
• Taxonomy: Isaurus is sometimes placed in family Parazoanthidae in older literature, but current treatment places it within Zoanthidae (Order Zoantharia).
breedingNotes: Reproduces via asexual budding in captivity. Well-fed colonies under stable water conditions have been reported to produce new polyps from the base of existing stalks. Sexual reproduction details in aquarium settings are not well documented.

additional.name: Reef Builders – Snake Polyps Are the Oddballs of the Coral World
additional.notes: Cross-checked behavioral traits (nocturnal extension, daytime limp posture), feeding requirements, and lighting. ReefBuilders recommends lower PAR and higher flow vs LiveAquaria's moderate lighting/medium flow. PAR range set at 75–200 to bridge the difference. Flow set to medium as a conservative average.
